role_summary: Osteopathic physician; founder and first president of the Albany Movement (1961-1962), the Georgia campaign that drew Martin Luther King Jr. and the SCLC; later first African American member and president of the American Osteopathic Association board of trustees.
ai_reading: Anderson's top semantic neighbor is H. Jack Geiger (entry 54, count 226), the Mississippi community-health-center founder; both are physicians whose movement work was anchored in medical practice as much as in protest organizing. The pairing extends to Alfred Moldovan (entry 133), Geiger's own top neighbor, and forms the catalog's clearest medical-civil-rights cohort signature. Beyond the medical cluster Anderson's neighbors are Wyatt Tee Walker (entry 132, SCLC), Charles McLaurin (entry 17, Mississippi SNCC), and Robert L. Carter (entry 108, NAACP LDF). The embedding reads him as a four-way bridge: medical organizing (Geiger, Moldovan), SCLC direct action (Walker, with whom Anderson partnered to invite King to Albany in 1961), SNCC field organizing (McLaurin), and NAACP legal work (Carter). This is the broadest cross-faction cluster the catalog has surfaced so far, consistent with the Albany Movement's specifically-coalitional founding (Anderson built it as a fusion of SNCC, SCLC, NAACP, and local groups) and a more distinctive signature than the cultural reading's "Albany Movement president" label captures.
biographical_paragraph: Anderson founded the Albany Movement in 1961 and served as its first president; the campaign drew SNCC field organizers, SCLC under Martin Luther King Jr., the NAACP, and the city's local Black organizations into one coalition that aimed at desegregating Albany, Georgia [src: 2]. In December 1961 he was imprisoned on charges related to an unlawful march attempting to hold prayers at city hall [src: 2]. He had been born December 12, 1927 in Americus, Georgia, took his Bachelor's at Alabama State University in 1949, and trained at Des Moines University College of Osteopathic Medicine [src: 2]. After the Albany Movement years he became the first African American to serve on the board of trustees of the American Osteopathic Association, where he later served as president; he was also associate dean at Kirksville College of Osteopathic Medicine and clinical professor at Michigan State University's College of Osteopathic Medicine, which hosts an annual civil rights lecture series in his name [src: 2]. His Library of Congress oral history is entry 126 of the Civil Rights History Project (American Folklife Center accession AFC 2010/039) [src: 1].

sources.title: William G. Anderson oral history
sources.publisher: Civil Rights History Project, Library of Congress (AFC 2010/039)

sources.title: Taylor Branch, Parting the Waters: America in the King Years, 1954-63 (the Albany Movement chapters cover Anderson's founding role)
William G. Anderson Civil Rights Lecture series (annual)
sources.publisher: Michigan State University College of Osteopathic Medicine
